note_data.title: Forces and motion
keyPoints: **Force** is a push or pull that can change the shape, size, or motion of an object
**Mass** is the amount of matter in an object and is measured in kilograms (kg)
**Weight** is the gravitational force acting on an object and is measured in newtons (N)
Weight = mass × gravitational field strength (W = mg)
**Scalar quantities** have magnitude only (mass, speed, distance, time)
**Vector quantities** have both magnitude and direction (force, weight, velocity, acceleration, momentum)
In free fall, all objects accelerate at approximately 10 m/s² due to gravity (ignoring air resistance)
**Momentum** = mass × velocity (p = mv) and is conserved in collisions
Terminal velocity is reached when air resistance equals weight, resulting in zero acceleration

definitions.definition: A push or pull that can change the shape, size, or motion of an object
The amount of matter in an object
The gravitational force acting on an object
The product of mass and velocity of a moving object
The constant velocity reached when the drag force equals the driving force

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Show the formula, substitute values, and give the final answer with correct units
Give clear differences between two concepts using 'whereas' or 'but'

modelAnswer.question: A cricket ball of mass 0.16 kg is dropped from the roof of a building in Lahore. Calculate the weight of the ball. (gravitational field strength = 10 N/kg)
modelAnswer.answer:
Weight = mass × gravitational field strength
W = mg
W = 0.16 × 10
W = 1.6 N
markBreakdown: Mark 3: Correct answer with units (1.6 N)

commonMistakes: Never say mass and weight are the same — mass is measured in kg, weight in N
Students often forget that weight changes with location but mass stays constant
Never say momentum is a scalar — it is a vector quantity with direction
Never forget units in calculations — weight must be in newtons (N)
examinerTips: Forces and motion appears in every Paper 2 — especially weight calculations
Always show the formula first, then substitute values for calculation questions
Remember to state direction when dealing with vector quantities
Free fall questions often test understanding that acceleration = 10 m/s² regardless of mass
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Free body diagram showing forces acting on a falling object: weight (downward arrow) and air resistance (upward arrow), with arrows drawn to scale to show relative magnitudes
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in past papers where students had to determine braking force from work done (Work = F × d, so 250 J = F × 18 m, giving F = 14 N)
Momentum-time graphs appear frequently - students must calculate resultant force from the gradient of the momentum-time graph using F = Δp/Δt
Equilibrium problems are common, such as finding air flow force on a deflected ball using force triangles and trigonometry (tan 30° = horizontal force/weight)
Force-extension graphs regularly test understanding of elastic limits, proportionality limits, and plastic deformation regions
Questions on elastic forces and tension in cords test Newton's third law and force pairs

workedExample.question: A ball of weight 0.15 N is suspended by a string in an air flow. The string makes an angle of 30° with the vertical. Calculate the horizontal force exerted by the air flow. (4 marks)
steps: Step 1: Draw a force diagram showing weight (0.15 N downward), tension (along string), and air force (horizontal)
Step 2: Resolve forces. For equilibrium: horizontal forces balance, vertical forces balance
Step 3: Horizontal equilibrium: Air force = T sin 30°. Vertical equilibrium: T cos 30° = 0.15 N
Step 5: Air force = T sin 30° = 0.173 × 0.5 = 0.087 N
workedExample.answer: The horizontal force exerted by the air flow is 0.087 N
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for force diagram, 1 mark for equilibrium conditions, 1 mark for correct calculation of tension, 1 mark for final answer

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused weight and mass in force calculations' — always use F = mg to convert mass to weight before applying Newton's laws
Examiner Report 2022: 'Students frequently forgot to resolve forces into components' — always break diagonal forces into horizontal and vertical components using trigonometry
Examiner Report 2024: 'Common error was not stating the direction of calculated forces' — every force answer must include both magnitude and direction for full marks
note_data.keyDiagram: Free body diagrams are essential - draw isolated objects with all forces as arrows from the center of mass. Label each arrow with force name and value. Use dotted lines to show resolved components. For equilibrium problems, draw force triangles showing how forces balance. Include scales and angles clearly marked.
note_data.quickRecap: Forces cause acceleration (F = ma). Objects in equilibrium have zero resultant force. Resolve diagonal forces using trigonometry (sin for opposite, cos for adjacent). Work done equals force times distance moved in direction of force. Always draw clear force diagrams and state directions in answers.
